past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed at age 19, occurring 2-3 times per month
Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed at age 21
Iron deficiency anemia, resolved after oral supplementation 2 years ago

medications.frequency: as needed for migraine, up to 9 tablets per month

family_history: Mother, age 52, has fibromyalgia and depression
No known family history of multiple sclerosis or other neurological disease

social_history.drugs: Occasional marijuana use socially, denies other illicit drugs
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a studio apartment near the university campus
social_history.exercise: Yoga 2 times per week, occasional hiking on weekends. Has been less active recently due to fatigue.
social_history.stress: Reports significant academic stress over the past year, especially around thesis deadlines

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody. No dysarthria. Attention intact to serial 7s. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No aphasia. Mood appears mildly anxious but affect is appropriate. PHQ-2 score 2 (mild).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally with correction. Visual fields full to confrontation. Pupils equal, round, reactive 4mm to 2mm bilaterally. No afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopic exam shows sharp disc margins bilaterally, no pallor and no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact in all directions of gaze. No nystagmus at primary gaze. Questionable 2-3 beats of unsustained horizontal nystagmus on extreme lateral gaze bilaterally, of uncertain significance. No diplopia reported. CN V: Sensation intact all three divisions bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. CN XI: Trapezius and sternocleidomastoid 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. No spasticity, rigidity, or clonus. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups of upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick intact. Vibration intact at bilateral great toes and medial malleoli. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. No dermatomal sensory loss. Patient reports subjective intermittent tingling in the left hand but no objective deficit identified on examination.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No Hoffman sign bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxia.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign not elicited. No Uhthoff phenomenon reported during examination. Patient appears fatigued but no objective findings to support a focal neurological deficit.

patient.chief_complaint: Episodic dizziness, fatigue, and intermittent tingling in the left hand over the past 6 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Jianjun Liu is a 22-year-old right-handed graduate student who was admitted for further workup after presenting to the outpatient neurology clinic with a six-month history of episodic vertigo, progressive fatigue, and intermittent tingling in his left hand. His symptoms began approximately six months ago when he first noticed brief episodes of room-spinning vertigo lasting 30 to 60 seconds, occurring two to three times per week, often triggered by quick head movements or standing up rapidly. He was initially evaluated by his primary care physician who diagnosed benign positional vertigo and prescribed meclizine, which provided minimal relief. Over the subsequent months, he developed a persistent sense of fatigue that he describes as unlike anything he has experienced before, not relieved by rest, and significantly impacting his ability to concentrate on his graduate coursework. He has also noticed intermittent tingling and a sensation of pins and needles in his left hand and fingers, occurring unpredictably, sometimes lasting minutes and occasionally persisting for several hours before resolving spontaneously. He reports that these sensory episodes do not follow a dermatomal pattern and are not associated with hand weakness or clumsiness. Approximately two months ago, he experienced a two-week period during which he felt unsteady on his feet, particularly when walking in dimly lit environments, but this resolved completely without intervention. He attributes much of his symptomatology to stress and sleep deprivation related to his thesis work. His primary care physician obtained a brain MRI after his most recent visit due to persistent symptoms, which was interpreted as showing several small non-specific white matter lesions, thought to be consistent with migraine-related changes given his history. He was referred to neurology for further evaluation and was admitted for a more comprehensive diagnostic workup including lumbar puncture. He denies any history of optic neuritis, limb weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, heat sensitivity, or electric shock-like sensations with neck flexion. He has had no recent fevers, weight loss, joint pain, oral ulcers, or skin rashes.

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, abutting the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: No spinal cord lesions on available sagittal sequences
Ventricles and sulci normal in size and configuration for age
mri.impression: Four scattered non-specific white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, predominantly subcortical. No enhancing lesions to suggest active inflammation. Does not meet full McDonald 2017 criteria for dissemination in space on this study alone. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 133 mEq/L (L), ANA 1:40 (low positive) titer (H), Vitamin D (25-OH) 21 ng/mL (L).

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Not detected in CSF or serum (matched pattern)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 29.2 mg/dL. Glucose: 59.5 mg/dL (ratio 0.70 (serum glucose 88 mg/dL)). oligoclonal_bands: Not detected in CSF or serum (matched pattern). IgG_index: 0.58 (normal: <0.70). IgG_synthesis_rate: -2.1 mg/day (normal: <8.0 mg/day). myelin_basic_protein: < 1.0 ng/mL (normal: <4.0 ng/mL). cytology: No malignant cells identified.

additional_observations: Mild disc desiccation at C5-C6, age-appropriate degenerative change
No cord compression or canal stenosis
Conus medullaris terminates at normal level (L1)
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with contrast. No intramedullary signal abnormalities or enhancement identified. No evidence of spinal cord demyelination. Mild degenerative changes at C5-C6 without cord compression. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, posterior horn of lateral ventricle
findings.borders: Ovoid, perpendicular to ventricle, classic Dawson finger morphology

findings.location: Right juxtacortical white matter, superior frontal gyrus

additional_observations: Two new lesions compared to prior study from 3 months ago
Prior lesions are stable in size
Now meets McDonald 2017 criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular and juxtacortical) and dissemination in time (new lesions on follow-up)
No infratentorial or spinal cord lesions identified on brain sequences
output.impression: Interval development of two new white matter lesions since prior MRI obtained 3 months ago. One new right juxtacortical lesion. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: Differentiating migraine white matter lesions from multiple sclerosis on MRI

results.title: MRI white matter lesions in migraine versus multiple sclerosis: a systematic review
results.key_finding: Migraine-related WML are typically small (<3mm), punctate, subcortical, and lack periventricular orientation. MS lesions tend to be larger, ovoid, periventricular with perpendicular orientation (Dawson fingers), and may enhance with gadolinium.

results.title: Diagnostic challenges in radiologically isolated syndrome and early MS
results.key_finding: Up to 10% of MS patients are OCB-negative at initial presentation. Serial MRI showing dissemination in time is critical when CSF is non-contributory. Periventricular lesion morphology (perpendicular to ventricle) has high specificity for MS even when few lesions are present.

results.title: McDonald 2017 criteria: practical application and common pitfalls
results.key_finding: A single periventricular lesion with Dawson finger morphology should raise suspicion for MS even if other criteria are not initially met. Follow-up imaging at 3-6 months is recommended when initial findings are inconclusive.

output.summary: Migraine-related white matter lesions are common but typically differ from MS lesions in size, morphology, and distribution. Periventricular lesions perpendicular to the ventricle (Dawson fingers) are more specific for MS. OCB-negative MS occurs in 5-10% of cases. When initial findings are equivocal, serial MRI demonstrating dissemination in time is a key diagnostic strategy per McDonald 2017 criteria.

differential.key_features: Known migraine history, subcortical lesion distribution on initial MRI, non-specific findings
Negative AQP4 antibodies, no longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis, no severe optic neuritis
Normal inflammatory markers, no systemic symptoms, normal CSF cell count
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no pulmonary findings, no elevated ACE

differential.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus with CNS involvement
differential.key_features: Low-titer ANA nonspecific, negative anti-dsDNA, no systemic lupus criteria met

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed clinical history focusing on prior neurological episodes, temporal pattern of symptoms, and red flag features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Episodic vertigo, fatigue, intermittent paresthesias, and a prior episode of gait unsteadiness — pattern of relapsing neurological symptoms disseminated in time
Examination largely normal or with subtle findings only; does not exclude MS in a diagnostic puzzle presentation

optimal_actions.action: Review brain MRI with attention to lesion morphology and distribution
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Non-specific white matter lesions, but one periventricular lesion with ovoid morphology perpendicular to the ventricle is suspicious for demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CSF entirely normal including absent oligoclonal bands, which occurs in 5-10% of MS patients

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, ANA, vitamin B12, TSH, ESR/CRP
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal except for nonspecific low-titer ANA and mild vitamin D insufficiency

optimal_actions.action: Order NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4) and MOG antibody testing to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal spinal cord MRI at this stage

optimal_actions.action: Consider visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic pathway involvement
Recommend follow-up MRI in 3 months to assess for dissemination in time given inconclusive initial workup
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New lesions including enhancing periventricular lesion with Dawson finger morphology and new juxtacortical lesion, now meeting McDonald 2017 criteria

optimal_actions.action: Establish diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS based on McDonald 2017 criteria fulfilled on follow-up imaging
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dissemination in space (periventricular + juxtacortical) and dissemination in time (new lesions on follow-up) now demonstrated

optimal_actions.action: Initiate disease-modifying therapy and refer for comprehensive MS care
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early initiation of DMT improves long-term outcomes in RRMS

critical_actions: Recognize that one periventricular lesion with ovoid perpendicular morphology on initial MRI is suspicious for demyelination despite overall non-specific appearance
Do not prematurely close on migraine as the sole explanation for white matter lesions
Order follow-up MRI in 3-6 months when initial findings are inconclusive for MS
Exclude NMOSD and MOGAD with appropriate antibody testing
Recognize that normal CSF (including absent oligoclonal bands) does not exclude MS
Obtain spinal cord MRI to assess for additional sites of demyelination
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing white matter lesions entirely as migraine-related without follow-up imaging
Starting disease-modifying therapy for MS before diagnostic criteria are met
Performing brain biopsy when clinical and imaging features are consistent with a demyelinating process
Initiating immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., cyclophosphamide) without a confirmed diagnosis
Attributing all symptoms to anxiety without adequate neurological investigation
key_reasoning_points: This case exemplifies the diagnostic challenge of early MS presenting with non-specific symptoms and MRI findings that overlap with migraine
A single periventricular lesion with Dawson finger morphology should not be dismissed even when the overall MRI appearance is non-specific
Normal CSF with absent oligoclonal bands occurs in approximately 5-10% of MS patients and should not rule out the diagnosis

The patient's history of distinct neurological episodes (vertigo, gait unsteadiness, paresthesias) separated in time raises suspicion for a relapsing-remitting process despite being attributed to benign causes
Serial MRI demonstrating dissemination in time is the critical diagnostic step when initial studies are inconclusive
The combination of known migraine history, anxiety disorder, and attributed stress creates anchoring bias that can delay MS diagnosis
Low-titer ANA is a common incidental finding and should not divert workup toward lupus in the absence of other criteria
Vitamin D insufficiency, while nonspecific, is epidemiologically associated with MS risk

teaching_points: Anchoring bias from pre-existing migraine diagnosis can delay MS recognition
OCB-negative MS is uncommon but well-documented
McDonald 2017 criteria can be fulfilled over time with serial imaging
Periventricular lesion morphology is a key discriminator between migraine and MS lesions
